task_completion.description: Verify the Sun is at the origin and Earth's orbit is a circle with radius 1.
task_completion.visual_evidence: In the GeoGebra Graphics view, there should be a point at the origin (0,0) representing the Sun. There should be a circle centered at (0,0) that passes exactly through 1 on the axes (radius 1), representing Earth's orbit. This should also be visible in the Algebra view as a circle equation like x^2 + y^2 = 1.

task_completion.description: Verify Mars's orbit is an ellipse with the Sun at one focus.
task_completion.visual_evidence: An ellipse should be drawn in the Graphics view. One of the two foci of this ellipse must be located exactly at the origin (0,0) where the Sun is positioned.

task_completion.description: Verify the dimensions of Mars's elliptical orbit match the specified semi-major axis and eccentricity.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The Algebra view should show the ellipse properties or points indicating the correct dimensions: semi-major axis of ~1.524 and focal distance (c) of ~0.1423. Visually, the ellipse should cross the axis at approximately 1.38 units on one side of the Sun (perihelion) and 1.66 units on the other side (aphelion).

task_completion.description: Verify the presence of 'Perihelion' and 'Aphelion' labels on Mars's orbit.
task_completion.visual_evidence: There must be distinct points on the Mars ellipse labeled 'Perihelion' (at the point closest to the origin) and 'Aphelion' (at the point farthest from the origin). The text labels must be clearly visible in the Graphics view.

task_completion.description: Verify the presence of a text label displaying the eccentricity value.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A text box or label must be visible in the GeoGebra Graphics view explicitly stating 'e = 0.0934' or containing the value 0.0934 in reference to the eccentricity.

task_completion.description: Verify the project is saved with the correct filename in the correct directory.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The GeoGebra window title should display 'mars_orbit.ggb', or a save dialog/terminal window should show the file 'mars_orbit.ggb' successfully saved in the '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/' directory.

integrity.description: Verify the agent used the GeoGebra GUI to construct the model.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots should show the GeoGebra application open with tools being used, rather than just a terminal window running a script to generate the .ggb file.

integrity.description: Verify the orbits are actual mathematical objects in GeoGebra.
integrity.visual_evidence: The Algebra view on the left side of the GeoGebra window should list the mathematical definitions (points, circles, ellipses, text) corresponding to the visual shapes, proving they are not just pasted images.

integrity.description: Verify the agent did not simply write XML into a .ggb archive manually.
integrity.visual_evidence: The presence of the GeoGebra interface with the constructed objects visible in the canvas, rather than a text editor modifying XML files.

privileged_info_for_vlm: Earth's orbit must be a circle centered at (0,0) with radius 1.0. Mars's orbit must be an ellipse with one focus at the Sun (0,0). Based on the required semi-major axis a=1.524 and eccentricity e=0.0934, the focal distance c (distance from center to focus) is 0.1423 AU. The perihelion (closest point to the Sun) is at a distance of 1.3816 AU, and the aphelion (farthest point) is at 1.6663 AU. The VLM should look for these approximate values in the GeoGebra Algebra view or coordinate axes to confirm accuracy. The final file must be named 'mars_orbit.ggb'.
